narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, LSAT score of 29, first-year school decile ranking of 1st (well below the average of around 6th), third-year decile of 3rd (also below average), and plans to attend full-time—and estimated a 65% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your low first-year decile ranking, as being in the bottom of your class that early signals potential struggles with the rigorous demands of legal training compared to peers who ranked much higher on average. Your undergraduate GPA, significantly below the typical 3.21 average, was the second strongest driver toward predicting failure, reinforcing concerns about your academic foundation in a field where strong grades are common predictors of bar success. Interestingly, your choice to attend full-time worked slightly in your favor, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing since most successful students (over 90%) follow this path and it allows more focus than part-time study. Overall, your metrics placed you below typical students in key academic areas, tipping the balance toward rejection despite your stable family income bracket. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character—many factors like motivation and study habits aren't captured here—but the model weighs early performance heavily. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 37 average, strengthening your undergrad record through a post-bac program or additional coursework, and seeking targeted tutoring to improve class rankings in future endeavors. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors to admission. You're taking a thoughtful step by understanding this feedback, and with focused improvements, law school remains within reach.
